24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (\cref{def:depth} and \cref{lem:depth_via_ext}),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (\cref{def:projective_dimension}),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (\cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (\cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (\cref{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.

61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  \uses{def:depth, lem:ext_smul_eq_zero_of_mem_annihilator}
110	
111	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
112	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
113	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
114	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
115	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
116	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
117	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
118	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
119	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
120	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
121	  \[
122	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
123	  \]
124	\end{lemma}
125	
126	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
127	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
128	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
129	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
130	%  By Lemma REF we have
131	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
132	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
133	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
134	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
135	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
136	%  Consider the long exact sequence
137	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
138	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
139	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
140	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
141	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
142	%  Lemma REF.
143	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
144	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
145	\begin{proof}
146	  
147	  
148	  
149	  
150	  
151	\leanok
152	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
153	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
154	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
155	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
156	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
157	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
158	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
159	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
160	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
161	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
162	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
163	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
164	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
165	\end{proof}

167	\section{Projective dimension}
168	\label{sec:ab_pd}
169	
170	\begin{definition}
171	
172	\leanok
173	  [Projective dimension]
174	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
175	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
176	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
177	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
178	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
179	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
180	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
181	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
182	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
183	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
184	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
185	  \[
186	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
187	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
188	  \]
189	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
190	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
191	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
192	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
193	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} concerns only nonzero \(M\).
194	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
195	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
196	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
197	\end{definition}
198	
199	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
200	\begin{itemize}
201	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
202	    iff \(M\) is free);
203	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
204	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
205	\end{itemize}
206	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
207	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
208	
209	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
210	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
211	
212	\begin{lemma}
213	
214	\leanok
215	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
216	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
217	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
218	  \uses{lem:natCast_add_one_le_of_le_sub_one}
219	
220	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
221	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
222	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
223	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
224	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
225	  %  \begin{enumerate}
226	  %  \item
227	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
228	  %  \item
229	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
230	  %  \item
231	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
232	  %  \end{enumerate}"
233	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
234	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
235	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
236	  \(R\)-modules. Then
237	  \begin{enumerate}
238	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
239	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
240	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
241	  \end{enumerate}
242	\end{lemma}
243	
244	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
245	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
246	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
247	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
248	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
249	%  from Lemma REF."
250	\begin{proof}
251	  
252	  
253	  
254	  
255	  
256	\leanok
257	  \uses{lem:depth_via_ext, lem:ext_vanish_of_natCast_lt_depth, lem:natCast_add_one_le_of_le_sub_one}
258	  By \cref{lem:depth_via_ext}, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
259	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
260	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
261	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
262	  \[
263	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
264	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
265	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
266	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
267	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
268	  \]
269	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
270	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
271	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
272	  the proof of \cref{lem:depth_via_ext}.
273	\end{proof}

275	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
276	\label{sec:ab_main}
277	
278	\begin{theorem}
279	
280	\leanok
281	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
282	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
283	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
284	  
285	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
286	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
287	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
288	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring. Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module
289	  %  which has finite projective dimension \(\text{pd}_R(M)\). Then we have
290	  %  \(\)
291	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
292	  %  \(\)"
293	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
294	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
295	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
296	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely
297	  generated \(R\)-module of finite projective dimension. Then
298	  \[
299	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
300	  \]
301	\end{theorem}
302	
303	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
304	% "We prove this by induction on \(\text{depth}(M)\). The most interesting

365	  \uses{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence, lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd,

367	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
368	
369	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
370	  finite free resolution
371	  \[
372	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
373	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
374	  \]
375	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
376	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
377	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
378	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
379	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
380	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
381	  sequences
382	  \[
383	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
384	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
385	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
386	  \]
387	  and applying \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence} iteratively,
388	  yields the chain of inequalities
389	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
390	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
391	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
392	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
393	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
394	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
395	
396	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
397	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
398	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
399	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
400	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
401	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
402	  \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
403	  \mathfrak{m}\( exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \)M$ as above; the snake
404	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
405	  \[
406	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
407	  \]
408	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
409	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
410	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
411	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
412	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
413	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
414	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
415	  \[
416	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
417	  \]
418	  Substituting yields
419	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
420	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
421	\end{proof}

636	\paragraph{Gap (2) Stacks 00MF proof recipe.} The
637	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
638	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}) reads: a complex
639	\(\dots \to R^{r_2} \xrightarrow{\varphi_2} R^{r_1}
640	 \xrightarrow{\varphi_1} R^{r_0}\) of finite free modules over a
641	Noetherian local ring \(R\) is exact at \(R^{r_i}\) (for \(i \geq 1\))
642	if and only if \(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi_{i+1} + \texttt{rank}\,\varphi_i
643	= r_i\) and \(\texttt{depth}\,I(\varphi_i) \geq i\), where
644	\(I(\varphi)\) denotes the ideal of \(r\)-minors of \(\varphi\) for
645	\(r = \texttt{rank}\,\varphi\). The recipe for an iter-201+ prover:
646	(a) define \(I(\varphi) \subseteq R\) as the ideal of all
647	\(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi\)-minors of \(\varphi\) (Mathlib supports
648	this via \(\texttt{Matrix.minors}\) and \(\texttt{Ideal.span}\) over the
649	finite index set of \(\binom{n}{r}\) tuples); (b) prove the depth
650	lower bound via induction on \(i\) using a Koszul-complex argument
651	on the regular sequence implicit in the exactness data; (c) derive
652	\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow \texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)
653	as a corollary by taking the start \(\dots \to R^{r_1} \to R^{r_0} \to
654	M\) of a minimal resolution and applying the criterion at \(i = 1\).
655	Estimated \(\sim 150-200\) LOC project-side; Mathlib upstream PR
656	candidate.

743	  Let \(R\) be a local ring (Noetherian-ness NOT required at this
744	  step), \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\) the maximal ideal,
745	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\) the residue field, and \(M\) a finite
746	  \(R\)-module. Then there exist \(n \in \mathbb N\) and an
747	  \(R\)-linear surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\) such that:
748	  \begin{enumerate}
749	    \item \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\) (the minimal
750	      generator count of \(M\), via Nakayama);
751	    \item \(\ker f \subseteq \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) (the
752	      ``minimality'' condition: every entry of every relation lives
753	      in the maximal ideal, so the chosen lift has no trivial
754	      summands).
755	  \end{enumerate}
756	\end{lemma}
757	\begin{proof}
758	
759	\leanok
760	  Pick a \(\kappa\)-basis
761	  \(b : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \kappa \otimes_R M\) via
762	  \texttt{Module.finBasis} with
763	  \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\). Surjectivity of the
764	  canonical map \(M \twoheadrightarrow \kappa \otimes_R M\)
765	  (composition of \texttt{TensorProduct.mk\_surjective} and
766	  \texttt{Ideal.Quotient.mk\_surjective}) gives lifts
767	  \(m_i \in M\) with \(1 \otimes m_i = b_i\). Define
768	  \(f : R^n \to M\) by \(f x := \sum_i x_i \cdot m_i\) via
769	  \texttt{(Pi.basisFun R (Fin n)).constr R m}.
770	
771	  Surjectivity: \(\mathrm{range}\, f = \mathrm{span}_R(m_i)\) by
772	  \texttt{Module.Basis.constr\_range}, and
773	  \(\mathrm{span}_R(m_i) = M\) by Nakayama via
774	  \texttt{IsLocalRing.span\_eq\_top\_of\_tmul\_eq\_basis}.
775	
776	  Kernel containment: for \(x \in \ker f\) the relation
777	  \(\sum_i x_i m_i = 0\) tensors to
778	  \(\sum_i (1 \otimes x_i \cdot m_i) = 0\); using
779	  \texttt{TensorProduct.tmul\_smul} and the linear independence of
780	  \(b\) (\texttt{Module.Basis.linearIndependent}) we obtain
781	  \(\mathrm{residue}_R(x_i) = 0\) for every \(i\), i.e.\
782	  \(x_i \in \mathfrak m\). Decomposing
783	  \(x = \sum_i x_i \cdot \mathrm{Pi.single}\,i\,1\) then yields
784	  \(x \in \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) by
785	  \texttt{Submodule.smul\_mem\_smul} and
786	  \texttt{Submodule.sum\_mem}.
787	\end{proof}
